mainmatter.p: A morphism { \bar {x} } \xrightarrow [ f ]{ \bar {f} }{ \bar {y} } \in E ^{ \mathsf {o} } is cartesian over { x } \xrightarrow {{ f }}{ y } if and only if the vertical leg of f is an isomorphism.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Suppose that { \bar {x} } \xrightarrow [ f ]{ \bar {f} }{ \bar {y} } is represented by a span { \mathopen {} \left \{ \bar {x} \leftarrow \bar {y} _{ x } \to \bar {y} \right \} \mathclose {}} in E in which the vertical leg \bar {x} \leftarrow \bar {y} _{ x } is an isomorphism. We must show that { \bar {x} } \xrightarrow [ f ]{ \bar {f} }{ \bar {y} } is cartesian in E ^{ \mathsf {o} }. We fix a morphism { \bar {w} } \xrightarrow [ m;f ]{ \bar {h} }{ \bar {y} } \in E ^{ \mathsf {o} } where { w } \xrightarrow {{ m }}{ x }, depicted below in terms of E ^{ \mathsf {o} }:
We must define the unique intervening map { \bar {w} } \xrightarrow [ m ]{ }{ \bar {x} } in E ^{ \mathsf {o} }. We first translate the above into the language of E by unfolding definitions:

embedded-tex.embedded-tex-body: \begin {tikzpicture}[diagram] \SpliceDiagramSquare <0/>{ west/style = lies over, east/style = lies over, north/style = {<-}, south/style = double, height = 1.5cm, nw = \bar {w}, sw = w, se = w, ne = \bar {y} _{ w } , north = \bar {h}, ne/style = pullback, } \SpliceDiagramSquare <1/>{ glue = west, glue target = 0/, east/style = lies over, height = 1.5cm, ne/style = pullback, se = x, ne = \bar {y} _{ x } \cong \bar {x}, south = m, } \SpliceDiagramSquare <2/>{ glue = west, glue target = 1/, east/style = lies over, height = 1.5cm, se = y, ne = \bar {y}, south = f, north = \bar {f}, } \end {tikzpicture}

block.p: The desired intervening map \bar {w} \xrightarrow [ m ]{} \bar {x} \in E ^{ \mathsf {o} } shakes out in the language of E to be a span { \mathopen {} \left \{ \bar {w} \xleftarrow [ 1 _{ w } ]{} \bar {y} _{ w } \xrightarrow [ m ]{} \bar {x} \right \} \mathclose {}} in which the left-hand leg is vertical and the right-hand leg is cartesian over { w } \xrightarrow {{ m }}{ x }. But the left-hand span { \mathopen {} \left \{ \bar {w} \xleftarrow [ 1 _{ w } ]{} \bar {y} _{ w } \to \bar {y} _{ x } \cong \bar {x} \right \} \mathclose {}} in the diagram above is exactly what we need.
We leave the converse to the reader.

frontmatter.title: Cartesian maps in the opposite category

mainmatter.p: There is a simple characterization of cartesian maps in E ^{ \mathsf {o} }.
